title: Frailty and Abuse of Older Adults: A Systematic Review of Empirical Studies
author: Klun, Monika
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Aim: This paper aims to systematically review the literature on the association between frailty and abuse of older adults, including abuse of frail older adults. Methods: According to the PRISMA Statement, the following databases were reviewed: CINAHL Ultimate, Cochrane Library, MEDLINE, ProQuest Ebook Central, PubMed, SAGE, Scopus, SpringerLink, Taylor and Francis Online, and Web of Science. The literature was reviewed by all authors, supplemented by backward snowballing and targeted manual searches in relevant journals. Empirical studies examining the relationship between frailty and elder abuse, including abuse of frail older adults, were included based on predefined eligibility criteria. Findings were synthesised using narrative synthesis approach, and study quality was assessed using Joanna Briggs Institute critical appraisal tools. The review protocol was not registered. Results: Four main themes were identified: prevalence of abuse of frail older adults, characteristics of victims of abuse of frail older adults, characteristics of perpetrators of abuse of frail older adults, and the association between frailty and abuse of older adults. The prevalence of abuse among frail older adults ranged from 17.9% to 43.9%. Victims were predominantly women and characterized by social isolation, sensory impairments, and anxiety. Perpetrators were typically family members and characterized by health problems, alcohol use, and caregiving burden. Most studies reported an association between frailty and abuse. Conclusions: The findings are heterogeneous across studies and should be interpreted with caution due to different methodological approaches. The available evidence does not allow conclusions regarding causality, underscoring the need for further longitudinal research.
steps_raw: Aim: This paper aims to systematically review the literature on the association between frailty and abuse of older adults, including abuse of frail older adults. Methods: According to the PRISMA Statement, the following databases were reviewed: CINAHL Ultimate, Cochrane Library, MEDLINE, ProQuest Ebook Central, PubMed, SAGE, Scopus, SpringerLink, Taylor and Francis Online, and Web of Science. The literature was reviewed by all authors, supplemented by backward snowballing and targeted manual searches in relevant journals. Findings were synthesised using narrative synthesis approach, and study quality was assessed using Joanna Briggs Institute critical appraisal tools. The review protocol was not registered. Results: Four main themes were identified: prevalence of abuse of frail older adults, characteristics of victims of abuse of frail older adults, characteristics of perpetrators of abuse of frail older adults, and the association between frailty and abuse of older adults. Perpetrators were typically family members and characterized by health problems, alcohol use, and caregiving burden. Most studies reported an association between frailty and abuse.
